mechanism_narrative: A high and rising unemployment rate signals deteriorating labor market conditions and weak aggregate demand, leading to lower corporate earnings expectations and negative forward returns for the S&P 500.
failure_condition: S&P 500 60-day forward returns are positive in more than 50% of instances when the unemployment rate is above 7.0%.

mechanism_narrative: A federal funds rate near the zero lower bound indicates maximum monetary policy accommodation, which lowers the discount rate for equity cash flows and provides a supportive backdrop for asset prices.
failure_condition: The hit rate for positive 20-day returns when FEDFUNDS < 0.25% falls below the unconditional baseline hit rate for a sustained period of 18 months.

mechanism_narrative: A concurrent slowdown in both real GDP and retail sales indicates a broad-based weakening of economic demand, negatively impacting corporate revenue forecasts and equity market performance.
candidate_trigger: Quarter-over-quarter GDPC1 growth is negative AND 3-month average RSAFS growth is negative.
evidence_refs: fact:High UNRATE suggests weak consumer backdrop
failure_condition: In periods where both proxies show negative growth, the S&P 500 60-day forward returns are positive more than 50% of the time.

mechanism_narrative: Sustained positive momentum in industrial production reflects a robust manufacturing and supply side of the economy, which translates directly to higher corporate profitability and drives positive equity returns.
failure_condition: The rank correlation between 3-month changes in INDPRO and 20-day forward S&P 500 returns is not statistically significant (p > 0.10) over a 5-year rolling window.

mechanism_narrative: In a high unemployment environment, accelerating nonfarm payrolls growth is a primary signal of economic recovery, boosting investor confidence and leading to strong positive equity returns.
failure_condition: This signal fails if, during periods matching the trigger condition, the S&P 500 produces negative 20-day returns more than 50% of the time.

mechanism_narrative: Rising inflation during a period of economic weakness (high unemployment) creates a stagflationary environment that compresses corporate profit margins and forces difficult policy trade-offs, leading to negative equity returns.
failure_condition: The strategy of shorting the S&P 500 when the trigger condition is met fails to produce a positive information ratio over a full business cycle.

mechanism_narrative: A sharp, negative surprise in housing starts data indicates an abrupt cooling in a key cyclical sector, signaling broader economic weakness and triggering a negative short-term market reaction.
candidate_trigger: Month-over-month HOUST declines by more than 10%.
failure_condition: The average 5-day S&P 500 return following a trigger event is not statistically significantly negative compared to the unconditional average (t-test p > 0.05).

mechanism_narrative: A sharp increase in nonfarm payrolls when the unemployment rate is already low signals an overheating labor market, increasing expectations for imminent monetary tightening and causing negative equity returns.
evidence_refs: fact:Phillips Curve trade-off between inflation and unemployment
failure_condition: In periods where the trigger condition is met, the S&P 500 has positive 20-day forward returns in more than 50% of instances.
